title: Aion Wire & Server — The Shared Wire Contract and the Standalone Deployable
intention: Build the network skin of Aion. The aion engine crate is transport-agnostic by design — no HTTP, gRPC, or WebSocket — so a CLI embeds it with nothing but a file-backed store. Teams running many services want a managed deployment: one binary clients and workers point at, that streams live workflow events to a dashboard, isolates tenants, and dispatches activity tasks to out-of-process workers in any language. That is aion-server, and the wire contract it speaks is aion-proto. aion-proto is a pure leaf-level contract — gRPC service definitions plus serde wire types — that the server, every client SDK (AL), and every worker SDK (AR) agree on, defined once so they never drift. aion-server is the deployable: it wraps a live Engine, translates wire requests into Engine calls, tails the engine's event broadcast to feed WebSocket subscribers, runs the server side of the remote-worker protocol, partitions everything by namespace, and serves the dashboard's API and static assets. It adds no execution logic — every durable decision still happens in aion.
problem: One wire contract, many speakers: a Python client, a TypeScript worker, and the Rust server must agree byte-for-byte on requests, activity-task dispatch, and how an Event serialises. The contract must be authored once in one leaf crate all three depend on, so a Python worker's build does not transitively pull the engine, beamr, or a store backend.

Translating the network onto a transport-agnostic API: the engine offers plain async Rust methods (start_workflow, signal, query, cancel, list_workflows, result, subscribe). Something must deserialise a wire request into the engine's aion-core parameter types, call the method, and serialise the result back — without reaching into engine internals or adding orchestration, or execution logic leaks into the server and violates the boundary the architecture rests on.

Real-time streaming from an append-only log: the engine appends an event and publishes it to an in-process broadcast channel surfaced as engine.subscribe(EventFilter). The server must let a remote client open a WebSocket, subscribe to a slice of that firehose (one workflow, a filtered class, or everything), receive events as they happen, and clean up on close — handling backpressure, slow consumers, and disconnect so a slow dashboard cannot stall the engine.

Out-of-process workers: Tier-3 activities run in workers in any language. The server is the rendezvous — workers connect, advertise activity types, receive task dispatches, execute in their own runtime, and report completion, failure, or heartbeat. The protocol is defined in aion-proto; the server side (accept connections, match tasks to workers, feed results into the engine's activity contract) lives here.

Multi-tenancy: a shared server serves many tenants. A namespace must isolate one tenant's workflows, events, and workers from another's — a client scoped to A cannot start/signal/query/list/stream a workflow in B, and a worker registered in A never receives a task from B.
Serving the dashboard: the dashboard UI is cluster AU. The server hosts it — serving the static asset bundle and backing it with the same API and WebSocket feed every other client uses.
None of these is execution logic. All are network, protocol, and operations — the work the engine deliberately refuses to do.

solution.summary: Two crates. aion-proto is the shared wire contract: gRPC service definitions (.proto) plus the serde request/response/event wire types they map to. It depends only on aion-core, so the wire Event and the domain Event are one type and never diverge, and it pulls in nothing else from the Aion tree — a clean leaf clients and workers target without the engine. aion-server is the standalone binary: it wraps a live aion::Engine, exposes it over gRPC + HTTP, streams events over WebSocket by tailing the engine's subscribe broadcast, runs the worker-protocol endpoint, enforces namespace isolation at the adapter boundary before any engine call, and serves the dashboard assets. It contains no workflow execution logic — it delegates every durable decision to the engine. The worker protocol rides gRPC bidirectional streaming with push dispatch and worker heartbeats. Wire errors are an explicit stable taxonomy mapped from typed engine/store errors. Config types that carry secrets are Deserialize-only per the credential policy; wire data types derive both.

decisions.title: Proto as the single shared wire contract
decisions.choice: The wire contract is one crate (aion-proto) depended on by the server, all client SDKs (AL), and all worker SDKs (AR), so the three categories of consumer cannot disagree on the wire form because there is exactly one definition. gRPC is the transport for the request/response API and the worker protocol; the .proto files are the machine-readable contract from which other-language SDKs generate stubs. The Rust side is hand-paired serde types mirroring the proto messages. Rejected: each SDK defining its own wire types — guaranteed drift and no single artefact for non-Rust codegen.

decisions.title: aion-proto depends on aion-core and nothing else in the tree
decisions.choice: aion-proto reuses aion-core's Event, WorkflowFilter, WorkflowSummary, WorkflowStatus, and ID newtypes directly (they already derive Serialize/Deserialize per aion-core CO6), so the wire Event is the domain Event. Proto messages carrying them are a thin envelope (namespace, request id, serialised core payload). aion-core is itself a leaf with no engine dependency, so a non-Rust worker's transitive graph stops at aion-core + aion-proto — never the engine, beamr, or a store. Rejected: a standalone aion-proto with cloned wire structs and a hand-maintained conversion layer — it reintroduces the multiple-definitions problem aion-core was created to prevent.

decisions.title: Wire errors are an explicit, stable taxonomy
decisions.choice: Every failure that crosses the wire becomes a WireError with a stable code and a human message, mapped from the engine's typed EngineError, the store's StoreError, query/signal failures, and namespace/authorisation failures. A client distinguishes 'workflow not found' from 'namespace denied' from 'sequence conflict' by code, not by parsing a string. Rejected: forwarding Debug-formatted engine errors — it leaks internals and gives clients nothing stable to branch on.

decisions.title: Secret-carrying config is Deserialize-only
decisions.choice: Per the project credential policy, aion-server's config types (store DSNs, TLS material, auth tokens) derive Deserialize but NOT Serialize, so a loaded credential cannot be accidentally re-serialised into a log, an error, or a response. Wire data types (proto messages, events, summaries) are ordinary data and derive both per aion-core CO6. The two are kept distinct.

decisions.title: The server is a pure adapter; no orchestration or execution
decisions.choice: A handler may deserialise, validate the namespace, call exactly one (or a small fixed composition of) Engine method(s), and serialise the result. It must not retry, schedule, sequence, or otherwise make a durable decision — those are the engine's, recorded in the event store. If a behaviour would change replay, it does not belong in aion-server. Rejected: convenience endpoints that batch or orchestrate multiple workflow operations with server-side logic — they would put un-recorded decision-making outside the engine.

decisions.title: A slow WebSocket consumer is dropped, never allowed to stall the engine
decisions.choice: Each connection has a bounded outbound buffer. If a client cannot keep up and the buffer fills, the connection is closed with a typed 'lagged' wire error rather than applying backpressure to the engine's broadcast (which would slow every other subscriber and the engine). The engine's broadcast tail is lossy-on-lag by construction; the server surfaces the lag as a typed disconnect so the client can resubscribe and re-read history if it needs the gap. Rejected: unbounded per-connection buffering — it turns one slow dashboard into an engine-wide memory and latency problem.

decisions.title: Worker protocol rides gRPC bidirectional streaming with push dispatch + heartbeats
decisions.choice: A worker opens a long-lived bidirectional gRPC stream: the server pushes ActivityTasks down it; the worker pushes ActivityResults and Heartbeats up it. Push (not Temporal-style task-queue polling) because the engine knows the moment an activity is scheduled and a connected worker is addressable — polling adds latency and wasted round-trips for no gain. gRPC bidi (not raw WebSocket) because the request/response API is already gRPC, so workers reuse one transport, auth path, and stub family. A missed-heartbeat window marks the worker lost and surfaces the task as failed to the engine. Rejected: task-queue polling (needless latency given push addressability) and WebSocket for workers (a second transport/codegen path for no benefit).

decisions.title: Namespace is the isolation unit, enforced at the adapter boundary before any engine call
decisions.choice: Every wire request carries a namespace; the server authorises the caller for it and scopes the operation to it before calling the engine. List/query/subscribe are filtered to the namespace; signal/query/cancel verify the target belongs to the caller's namespace; worker registration and dispatch are partitioned by namespace. Whether namespaces map to separate Engine/store instances or one shared engine with a namespace key is a deploy-time choice abstracted behind a NamespaceResolver; the enforcement — no cross-namespace operation reaches the engine — is invariant either way. Rejected: trusting clients to stay in their namespace, or filtering only on the way out.

decisions.title: Server feeds remote results into the engine's activity contract; recording and retry stay the engine's
decisions.choice: When a worker returns an ActivityResult, the server hands it to the engine's activity contract so the engine records ActivityCompleted/ActivityFailed and resumes the workflow — exactly as for in-VM activities (aion-engine D7). A worker that disconnects or stops heartbeating before reporting causes the engine to treat the activity as failed per its retry policy; the server reports the lost worker, it does not itself decide the retry. Rejected: the server deciding retries or recording activity events — that duplicates engine logic and puts event-sourcing decisions outside the engine.

decisions.title: The dashboard is just another client of the public contract
decisions.choice: The server serves cluster AU's built static asset bundle from a configured (or embedded) path and backs it with the same gRPC/HTTP handlers and the same WebSocket event feed every other client uses. There is no dashboard-specific API — anything the dashboard can do, any client can do. This keeps the server honest and avoids a privileged side channel.

constraints.description: No #[allow]/#[expect]/#[ignore] lint bypasses per CLAUDE.md; tests needing a running server gate at runtime (a *_TEST_URL env var with a logged skip), never #[ignore].
lib.rs/mod.rs are declarations and re-exports only.
aion-proto is a leaf crate: it depends on aion-core and external crates only — no aion, no aion-server, no store backend. Structural; must hold (per D2).
aion-server depends on aion, aion-proto, and a store backend among workspace crates; it contains no workflow execution logic — it delegates every durable decision to the Engine (per D5).
Wire data types (proto messages, streamed events, summaries) derive Serialize + Deserialize; config types that carry secrets are Deserialize-only (D4, project credential policy).
Library errors use thiserror (WireError in proto, ServerError in server); anyhow appears only in aion-server's top-level main. No unwrap/expect in library code; lock/stream poison handled explicitly.
No cross-namespace operation reaches the engine: namespace authorisation and scoping happen at the adapter boundary before any Engine call (per D8).
A slow or stalled WebSocket consumer is dropped with a typed lag error; per-connection buffers are bounded; the engine broadcast is never back-pressured by a subscriber (per D6).
No hardcoded operational defaults (ports, heartbeat window, buffer bound, timeouts): all from config; engine-owned defaults deferred to the engine (per CLAUDE.md 'no assumed defaults').
The wire Event/WorkflowFilter/WorkflowSummary/WorkflowStatus and ID types are aion-core's types, not re-declared wire clones (per D2).

goals: aion-proto defines, once, the gRPC service + serde wire types for workflow management, event streaming, the worker protocol, and a WireError taxonomy, depending only on aion-core.
Proto messages reuse aion-core's Event/WorkflowFilter/WorkflowSummary/WorkflowStatus/ID types via a thin envelope; no wire clones; lossless proto<->core conversion.
WireError maps EngineError, StoreError, query/signal, and namespace/auth failures onto stable language-neutral codes.
aion-server wraps a live Engine and exposes start/signal/query/cancel/list/describe over gRPC and an HTTP/JSON facade sharing one handler layer.
The server is a pure adapter — each handler deserialises, scopes the namespace, calls the Engine, serialises the result; no orchestration or durable decision.

WebSocket streaming: a SubscriptionRequest (per-workflow, filtered, firehose) maps to an EventFilter; a per-connection task tails engine.subscribe and forwards events; close/lag drops the subscription cleanly.
A slow WebSocket consumer is dropped with a typed lag error and bounded buffers; the engine broadcast is never back-pressured.

The remote-worker endpoint accepts gRPC bidi connections: RegisterWorker, push ActivityTask, collect ActivityResult/Heartbeat, feed results into the engine's activity contract, mark missed-heartbeat workers lost.
Namespace isolation enforced at the adapter boundary before any engine call, abstracted behind a NamespaceResolver; no cross-namespace operation reaches the engine.
The server serves cluster AU's static dashboard bundle and backs it with the same public API and WebSocket feed; no privileged dashboard API.
Deserialize-only config types (store DSN, ports, TLS, auth, heartbeat window, buffer bound, dashboard path, namespace mode); a thin main with anyhow only at the top level.
non_goals: No workflow execution logic — lifecycle, replay, timers, signals, queries, concurrency all live in aion (AE/AD/AT). This cluster translates the network onto the Engine and adds nothing executable.

No engine event-broadcast implementation — the engine appends/publishes events and exposes subscribe(EventFilter) (AE/AD). This cluster tails that broadcast over WebSocket; it does not implement the broadcast.
No worker-side SDKs — the Rust/Python/TS workers are cluster AR. This cluster defines the worker protocol in aion-proto and the server-side endpoint only.
No caller client SDKs — the Python/TS/Gleam/Rust clients are cluster AL. This cluster defines the API in aion-proto only.
No dashboard frontend — the dashboard UI is cluster AU. This cluster serves AU's built assets and backs them with the public API; it builds no UI.
No storage backend — libSQL is AS, PostgreSQL is AX. The server selects a backend at deploy time and treats the store as an opaque EventStore.
No distributed coordination / sharding — a later concern; this cluster targets the single-server standalone deployment and namespace isolation within it.

structure.crates/aion-proto/src/lib.rs: [AW-001] thin re-export surface + include! of generated output
structure.crates/aion-server/src/worker/dispatch.r: [AW-011] match task -> worker, feed result to engine
